title: LSA Data Analysis
text:
Every attempt recorded on the Evaluation Chart, analyzed: how evenly time is spread across students, and what percentage of the class has reached its own potential on each criterion (Gordon’s “move ahead” rule of thumb is 80%).

  

  

  
    Attempts per student
    The equal-time proxy the "suggest next" prompt optimizes for — the tighter the spread, the more even the class's time has been.

title: LSA Directory
text:
Sequential organization of Learning Sequence Activities (LSAs) from the Jump Right In tonal and rhythm registers, by unit, skill, and content/context. Unit/skill/content source: LSA-TOC.pdf. Criteria are parsed live from the pattern libraries at apps/patterns/pattern-object.js and apps/rhythms/rhythm-pattern-object.js — a key like t1-1A1 is Tonal Register Book 1, Unit 1, Section A, Criterion 1.
Click a criterion chip (e.g. A1) to open that criterion in the evaluation chart. Units with no chips haven’t been digitized into the pattern library yet.



  Tonal Register
  
  

  Rhythm Register

title: LSA Instructions
text:
Every digitized LSA criterion, in one table, with the instruction text transcribed from its source page (the line just above the E/M/D patterns — e.g. “Students sing patterns using tonal syllables”). Tonal Register Book 1 instructions come from JRI Tonal Register Analysis.docx; the other three books were read directly off the rendered PDF pages. Click a row’s key to open that criterion in the Evaluation Chart.



  
  
    All skills
  
  
    All content / context

title: LSA Practice (Notated)
text:
Work through Learning Sequence Activities on your own, continuously — a few class patterns to chant along with before each individual pattern, then your own turn, genuinely graded by ear (just like the rhythm assessment tool), following along with the notation for each pattern. Results stay hidden; nothing is saved. (Test scope: only Rhythm Unit 1 is wired up so far.)

title: Lesson: The Quarter Note
text: The quarter note gets one beat in common time. In a 4/4 measure you can fit four of them — one per beat. We count them simply: 1 – 2 – 3 – 4.

title: Lesson: The Quarter Note
text:
Hear it, then say it
Before reading a single quarter note, you should be able to audiate a steady beat — feel it internally — and chant a neutral syllable on each beat. Only after the sound is secure do we attach the symbol.

title: Lesson: The Quarter Note
text:
Practice steps

Tap a steady beat with your foot.
Chant “bah” on each tap for four beats.
Now look at four quarter notes and chant them while tapping.

When you’re ready, head to Practice → Rhythm Patterns and play a few quarter-note patterns. Your time on this page and your plays over there are both recorded automatically.






Tip



This page is being timed. Stay as long as you need; switch tabs and the timer pauses. Come back and it resumes.

title: Lesson: Do, Re, Mi (Tonal Patterns)
text: Tonal patterns are short groups of pitches we learn by ear first, with solfège, before we ever name a key signature. The foundation is the major tonic and dominant.

title: Lesson: Do, Re, Mi (Tonal Patterns)
text:
Stepwise motion
The most basic tonal pattern is stepwise: Do – Re – Mi – Fa – Sol and back down. Sing it on a neutral syllable first, then with solfège, then connect it to what you see on the staff.

title: Lesson: Do, Re, Mi (Tonal Patterns)
text:
Tonic and dominant

Tonic (Do – Mi – Sol): the “home” chord, restful.
Dominant (Sol – Ti – Re): the “tension” chord that wants to resolve home.

Hearing the difference between these two is the single most important tonal skill in beginning band.

title: Lesson: Do, Re, Mi (Tonal Patterns)
text:
Practice steps

Sing Do, then find Sol.
Sing the tonic arpeggio, then the dominant.
Go to Practice → Tonal Patterns and play major stepwise patterns, singing along with solfège.

title: Accompaniment Styles
text:
These patterns are built into the accompaniment engine and can be selected on any tune page.
    Each style adapts automatically to the tune's meter and chord chart.
    Styles marked any meter work in 2/4, 3/4, 4/4, and 6/8; fixed-meter styles
    fall back to block chords when the meter doesn't match. Examples shown in C major at 110 bpm.
  

  
    By genre
    By instrument
  

  
  

  
  

    
      Jazz
      

        
          
            Jazz Charleston
            charleston
            4/4
            ▶
          
          
          Chord on beat 1 and the and-of-2, leaving beats 3 and 4 open. Named for the 1920s dance; the defining syncopation of early jazz and Swing Era big-band writing.
          
            Jazz
            Swing
            Piano
            Full band
          
        

        
          
            Jazz waltz
            jazzwaltz
            3/4
            ▶
          
          
          Swung bass on beat 1, ride-cymbal implied chord on beat 3. The feel of bebop and post-bop piano trios — Evans, Peterson, Monk in 3/4.
          
            Jazz
            Bebop
            Piano trio
          
        

        
          
            Walking bass
            walk
            4/4
            ▶
          
          
          Bass moves in step-wise quarter notes through chord tones and approach notes; right-hand comping on beats 2 and 4 (Charleston rhythm). The defining texture of jazz trio and big-band rhythm-section playing from bebop onward.
          
            Jazz
            Bebop
            Piano
            Bass
          
        

      
    

    
      Latin
      

        
          
            Bossa nova
            bossa
            4/4
            ▶
          
          
          Tresillo-derived bass pattern with chords on the off-beats; ride cymbal and side-stick percussion. Rooted in the guitar style of João Gilberto and the samba-jazz fusion of late-1950s Rio.
          
            Latin
            Jazz
            Brazilian
            Guitar
            Piano
          
        

        
          
            Tango
            tango
            4/4
            ▶
          
          
          Habanera rhythmic cell in the bass (long–short–short–long) with syncopated chords. Descended from Cuban contradanza via the Argentine arrabal — the tresillo pattern that underlies both the habanera and early tango.
          
            Latin
            Argentine
            Cuban
            Bandoneón
            Piano
          
        

        
          
            Son montuno
            montuno
            4/4
            ▶
          
          
          Cuban piano tumbao: bass-chord-chord repeating against the clave. The foundational piano pattern of son cubano and salsa; the rhythmic motor behind virtually all Afro-Cuban popular music.
          
            Latin
            Cuban
            Salsa
            Piano
          
        

        
          
            Cha-cha
            chacha
            4/4
            ▶
          
          
          Bass on beat 1, chord on 2, then three quick chords on "3–and–4" — the "1·2·cha-cha-chá" count. Evolved from the mambo in 1950s Cuba; named for the shuffling footwork of dancers.
          
            Latin
            Cuban
            Piano
            Full band
          
        

        
          
            Calypso / mento
            calypso
            4/4
            ▶
          
          
          Bass on beats 1 and 3, light chords on every upbeat — the Trinidadian and Jamaican "afterbeat." Mento is the Jamaican root form; calypso developed in Trinidad. Both patterns flowed into ska and reggae.
          
            Latin
            Caribbean
            Roots
            Guitar
            Piano
          
        

        
          
            Samba
            samba
            4/4
            ▶
          
          
          Surdo-style kick on beats 2 and 4 (absent on beat 1), snare on beat 3, hi-hat fills every eighth. The rhythmic foundation of Brazilian carnival parade music; transferred to the drum kit from the samba bateria.
          
            Latin
            Brazilian
            Drum set
            Bateria
          
        

        
          
            Mambo
            mambo
            4/4
            ▶
          
          
          Kick on beat 1 and the "and-of-3" (a clave-derived anticipation), snare on beat 3, hi-hats throughout. Developed in 1940s Havana by Dámaso Pérez Prado; bridged Cuban son and American big-band.
          
            Latin
            Cuban
            Drum set
            Full band
          
        

      
    

    
      Funk & R&B
      

        
          
            Funk
            funk
            4/4
            ▶
          
          
          Kick on beats 1 and 3; syncopated snare on the "and-of-2" and "and-of-4"; hi-hat anchors the eighth-note pulse. James Brown's drummers — Clyde Stubblefield and John "Jabo" Starks — defined this feel.
          
            Funk
            R&B
            Drum set
          
        

        
          
            Half-time feel
            halftime
            4/4
            ▶
          
          
          Snare moves to beat 3 (rather than 2 and 4), making 2 bars feel like 1. Common in hip-hop, stadium rock, and ballad bridges where the tempo needs to feel broader without actually slowing down.
          
            Pop
            Hip-hop
            Rock
            Drum set
          
        

        
          
            New Orleans second line
            secondline
            4/4
            ▶
          
          
          Kick on every beat; snare on the "and-of-1" and "and-of-3." The marching parade groove of New Orleans brass bands; adopted by jazz funk, R&B, and rock drummers (the Meters, Allen Toussaint, Dr. John).
          
            Funk
            New Orleans
            Jazz
            Drum set
            Marching
          
        

      
    

    
      Waltz & Dance
      

        
          
            Waltz
            waltz
            3/4
            ▶
          
          
          Bass on beat 1, chord on beats 2 and 3. The universal dance-band formula for 3/4 time. The slight delay before the chord creates the characteristic lilt that distinguishes waltz from a march.
          
            Dance
            Classical
            Piano
            Orchestra
          
        

        
          
            Viennese waltz
            viennese
            3/4
            ▶
          
          
          A short downbeat chord followed by slightly-early beats 2 and 3 (the "anticipated" Viennese lilt). The elastic timing distinguishes the Strauss concert hall waltz from the plainer ballroom waltz.
          
            Dance
            Classical
            Romantic
            Orchestra
            Piano
          
        

        
          
            Boom-chick
            oompah
            any
            ▶
          
          
          Root on the downbeat, chord on every other beat. The engine of polka, march, and German dance-band styles; derived from tuba–horn interplay in brass ensembles.
          
            Folk
            Polka
            March
            Brass
            Accordion
          
        

      
    

    
      Folk & Country
      

        
          
            Folk — alternating bass
            folk
            any
            ▶
          
          
          Alternating root–fifth bass note with a chord on the off-beat. The defining guitar and banjo accompaniment pattern in American folk, bluegrass, and country; also called the "Carter scratch."
          
            Folk
            Country
            Bluegrass
            Guitar
            Banjo
          
        

      
    

    
      Pop & Rock
      

        
          
            Pop / ballad
            pop
            any
            ▶
          
          
          Bass on beats 1 and 3, steady quarter-note chords on all beats. The most common piano and keyboard pattern in popular music since the 1950s.
          
            Pop
            Rock
            Piano
            Keyboard
          
        

        
          
            Driving 8ths
            driving8
            any
            ▶
          
          
          Steady eighth-note chords with no gaps. Propulsive and energetic; fills every subdivision. Found across rock, country, and pop guitar — sometimes called a "strum pattern."
          
            Rock
            Country
            Pop
            Guitar
            Piano
          
        

        
          
            Offbeat chords
            offbeat
            any
            ▶
          
          
          Chord only on the off-beats, silence on the downbeats. The defining rhythmic gesture of ska and reggae; known as the "skank" or "chop." In reggae, the kick lands on beat 3 (the "one-drop").
          
            Ska
            Reggae
            Guitar
            Keyboard
          
        

      
    

    
      Classical & Piano
      

        
          
            Block chords
            block
            any
            ▶
          
          
          All voices together on every beat, sustained until the next chord. The simplest possible accompaniment; used for chorale writing, teaching chord shapes, and very slow tempos.
          
            Classical
            Chorale
            Piano
            Keyboard
          
        

        
          
            Piano arpeggio ↑
            arpflow
            4/4
            ▶
          
          
          Rising arpeggios through the full bar (root–third–fifth–octave). Characteristic of Romantic-era piano writing and nocturne accompaniment; adds forward motion when chord changes are sparse.
          
            Classical
            Romantic
            Piano
          
        

        
          
            Arpeggio (root–5th)
            arpcell
            any
            ▶
          
          
          Root and fifth as individual tones in a repeating per-beat cell. Creates a light, open texture that suggests harmonic motion without dense block chords; useful for harp or guitar figuration.
          
            Classical
            Pop
            Harp
            Guitar
            Piano
          
        

        
          
            Bass line (roots)
            bassroot
            any
            ▶
          
          
          Root note only in the bass, no chord voices above. Use as a harmonic foundation when the melody instrument carries the full harmonic content, or when learning to hear bass motion in isolation.
          
            Teaching
            Minimal
            Bass
          
        

      
    

  

  
  

    
      Piano & Keyboard
      
        
          Block chordsblockany
          Sustained block voicing on every beat. The starting point for learning chord shapes and for chorale-style writing.
          ClassicalChorale
        
        
          Waltzwaltz3/4
          Left-hand bass on 1, right-hand chord on 2–3. The standard piano waltz texture from Chopin through the ballroom repertoire.
          DanceClassical
        
        
          Pop / balladpopany
          Bass on 1 and 3, chords on every beat. Ubiquitous in pop piano; easy to adapt to either hands-together or stride-style playing.
          PopRock
        
        
          Piano arpeggio ↑arpflow4/4
          Rising broken chord through the full bar. Nocturne and ballad texture; standard left-hand figure in Romantic piano writing.
          ClassicalRomantic
        
        
          Viennese waltzviennese3/4
          Elastic 3/4 with the characteristic early-beat anticipation. Strauss orchestra and salon piano repertoire.
          DanceClassical
        
        
          Jazz Charlestoncharleston4/4
          Syncopated 4/4 comping with a chord on beat 1 and the and-of-2. Common in Stride, Swing, and mainstream jazz piano.
          JazzSwing
        
        
          Jazz waltzjazzwaltz3/4
          Swung bass on 1, light chord on 3. The texture of jazz piano trios playing in 3 — Evans, Peterson, Monk.
          JazzBebop
        
      
    

    
      Guitar & Plucked Strings
      
        
          Folk — alternating bassfolkany
          Thumb alternates root–fifth in the bass; fingers strum the chord on the off-beat. The foundational fingerpicking and flatpicking pattern in folk, bluegrass, and country guitar.
          FolkCountryBluegrass
        
        
          Driving 8thsdriving8any
          Steady downstroke eighth notes, no rests. The strum pattern behind countless rock, country, and pop songs.
          RockCountryPop
        
        
          Offbeat chordsoffbeatany
          The ska/reggae "skank": chord only between the beats. In reggae the downstroke is muted, leaving only the upstroke chop.
          SkaReggae
        
        
          Bossa novabossa4/4
          Tresillo bass with off-beat chords. João Gilberto's original bossa guitar part is the blueprint; the pattern is also widely adapted to keyboard.
          LatinBrazilianJazz
        
        
          Arpeggio (root–5th)arpcellany
          Open-voiced ascending tones per beat. Evokes harp, classical guitar, and lute figuration; widely used in Renaissance and Baroque lute tablature.
          ClassicalPop
        
      
    

    
      Brass & Ensemble
      
        
          Boom-chickoompahany
          Tuba carries the root on 1; horns answer with the chord on 2 (and 4). The pattern maps directly to the brass section interplay in polka bands, march bands, and German Blasmusik.
          PolkaMarchFolk
        
        
          Tangotango4/4
          Habanera cell in the bass with syncopated upper chords. In Argentine tango this interplay moves between bandoneón and piano; in orchestral tango (Piazzolla) it drives the entire ensemble.
          LatinArgentine
        
      
    

    
      Bass & Foundation
      
        
          Bass line (roots)bassrootany
          Root note only, no chords. Use this when practicing bass lines in isolation, or when the melody is dense enough to imply the harmony on its own.
          TeachingMinimal
        
      
    

    
      Drum Set
      
        
          Backbeatbackbeat4/4
          Kick on beats 1 and 3, snare on 2 and 4. The universal pop and rock groove; the first drum pattern most students encounter.
          PopRock
        
        
          Driving 8thsdriving84/4
          Continuous eighth notes on the hi-hat with backbeat snare. Energetic pop and rock feel; common in anthemic and uptempo styles.
          PopRock
        
        
          Ska / reggae (one-drop)ska4/4
          Kick displaced to beat 3; snare and hi-hat emphasise the off-beats. The rhythmic foundation of ska, rocksteady, and roots reggae.
          ReggaeSka
        
        
          Swing — ride cymbalswing_ride4/4
          Swung ride pattern with kick and snare on beats 2 and 4. The standard jazz ride pattern from Swing through bebop.
          JazzSwing
        
        
          Swing — hi-hatswing_hats4/4
          Swung quarter-note hi-hat pulse; lighter texture than ride-cymbal swing. Used in small-group jazz and ballad settings.
          JazzBallad
        
        
          Swing — open hi-hatswing_hats_open4/4
          Swung hi-hat opening on beats 2 and 4; airy, washy texture. Common in contemporary jazz and fusion where the drummer wants more cymbal wash than the closed-hat version.
          JazzFusion
        
        
          Swing — brushesswing_brushes4/4
          Wire-brush swirl with soft backbeat. Quiet jazz trio and intimate club setting; the standard brush technique for standards and ballads.
          JazzBallad
        
        
          Jazz waltz — ridejazzwaltz_ride3/4
          Swung ride in 3; kick on beat 1, light snare elsewhere. The jazz-waltz drum feel behind Bill Evans, Miles Davis, and the post-bop trio repertoire.
          JazzWaltz
        
        
          Jazz waltz — hi-hatjazzwaltz_hats3/4
          Closed hi-hat pulse in 3 with a light swing feel; more understated than the ride-cymbal version. Used in intimate trio settings where the drummer wants less projection.
          JazzWaltz
        
        
          Bossa novabossa4/4
          Ride cymbal tresillo with side-stick; the drum kit adaptation of Brazilian percussion. Foundational in bossa nova and samba-jazz.
          LatinBossa
        
        
          Tango (habanera)tango4/4
          Habanera kick cell with closed hi-hat. The rhythmic engine of Argentine tango and early-20th-century Latin dance music.
          LatinTango
        
        
          Son montuno (clave)montuno4/4
          Cuban clave rhythm on the kick; snare and hi-hat fill the groove. The drum kit version of the son montuno pattern.
          LatinCuban
        
        
          Waltzwaltz3/4
          Kick on beat 1, hi-hat on 2 and 3. Straightforward triple-meter groove for waltzes, country, and folksong accompaniment.
          DanceFolk
        
        
          Compound (6/8)compound66/8
          Kick on the two main beats, hi-hat subdivisions filling the compound pulse. Used in jigs, compound-feel pop, and gospel 6/8.
          FolkGospel
        
        
          Half-time feelhalftime4/4
          Snare on beat 3 only; kick and hi-hat fill the rest. Makes the bar feel twice as wide without changing tempo. Widely used in hip-hop, stadium rock, and ballad bridges.
          PopHip-hopRock
        
        
          Funkfunk4/4
          Kick on beats 1 and 3; syncopated snare on the "and-of-2" and "and-of-4"; hi-hat on all eighths. The foundational James Brown / Clyde Stubblefield groove.
          FunkR&B
        
        
          New Orleans second linesecondline4/4
          Kick on every beat, snare on the upbeats of 1 and 3. The marching street-parade groove; adopted by the Meters, Dr. John, and rock drummers worldwide.
          FunkNew Orleans
        
        
          Sambasamba4/4
          Surdo kick on beats 2 and 4; snare on beat 3; hi-hat throughout. The drum kit adaptation of the samba bateria; common in Brazilian jazz and fusion.
          LatinBrazilian
        
        
          Mambomambo4/4
          Kick on beat 1 and the "and-of-3" (a clave-derived anticipation); snare on beat 3. The driving groove of 1940s Havana; ancestor of modern salsa.
          LatinCuban
        
        
          Calypso / mentocalypso4/4
          Floating kick (no downbeat on 1); side-stick on beat 3; hi-hat emphasises the upbeats. The Trinidadian and Jamaican afterbeat; ancestral to ska and reggae.
          CaribbeanReggae
        
      
    

    
      Hand Percussion
      
        
          Bongo — martillobongo_martillo4/4
          Steady eighth-note alternation between hi and lo bongo; the "hammer" pattern. The default timekeeping groove in Cuban son and salsa.
          LatinCuban
        
        
          Bongo — quarterbongo_slow4/4
          Quarter-note hi/lo alternation; a relaxed, open texture. Works well as a simplified bongo part for classroom or beginner settings.
          LatinTeaching
        
        
          Conga — tumbaoconga_tumbao4/4
          Open tone on the and-of-4, muted tones elsewhere. The foundational Cuban conga pattern; ubiquitous in salsa and son.
          LatinCuban
        
        
          Conga — tumbao (dense)conga_tumbao_dense4/4
          Eight-note tumbao with slap and open tone variations; a fuller, more active feel than the standard four-note version.
          LatinCuban
        
        
          Conga — sonconga_son4/4
          Alternating lo-conga open tones across the bar; simpler and more open than the tumbao. Common in son cubano and early salsa.
          LatinCuban
        
        
          Conga — waltzconga_waltz3/4
          Conga tones on the three main beats in 3/4; pairs with Latin waltz and compound-feel songs.
          LatinWaltz
        
        
          Conga — 6/8conga_6_86/8
          Open and muted conga tones across the compound-triple pulse. Works with 6/8 Afro-Cuban styles and compound jigs.
          LatinAfro-Cuban

title: Music Practice
text:
Rhythm, melody, and song — tools for music students from kindergarten through
  high school. Practice on your own, or follow a session your teacher has set up.


Practice

  
    Rhythm
    Rhythm Sequences
    Build rhythmic vocabulary pattern by pattern.
  
  
    Melody
    Tonal Sequences
    Practice melodic patterns by ear and by sight.
  
  
    Composition
    Song Builder
    Assemble rhythm and melody cells into a short piece.
  
  
    Melody
    Tonal Patterns
    Stepwise and harmonic patterns, major and minor.
  
  
    Rhythm
    Rhythm Patterns
    Notated rhythm reading and performance.
  


Assessment

  
    Pitch & Rhythm
    Tune Assessment
    Perform a tune; get scored on pitch and rhythm accuracy.
  
  
    Melody
    Tonal Accuracy
    Sing or play back tonal patterns on demand.
  
  
    Rhythm
    Rhythmic Accuracy
    Perform rhythm patterns and see your timing.
  


Teacher tools

  
    Assign
    Session Builder
    Write a guided practice session and share the link.
  
  
    Review
    Teacher Report
    Time on task, plays, and note interactions by student.

title: Lesson: Dotted Rhythms
text: A dot adds half the value of the note again. A dotted half note (normally two beats) becomes three beats. A dotted quarter becomes one-and-a-half.

title: Lesson: Dotted Rhythms
text:
The dotted-quarter-plus-eighth figure
The classic figure is a dotted quarter followed by an eighth — the “long- short” pattern you hear at the start of countless tunes. It’s tricky because the eighth note lands on the and of beat two, not on a beat.

title: Lesson: Dotted Rhythms
text:
Practice steps

Chant a steady “1 & 2 & 3 & 4 &”.
Hold the dotted quarter across “1 & 2”, then say the eighth on “&”.
Try it slowly, then bring it up to tempo.

Then go to Practice → Tunes and find a tune that uses this figure. Play it a few times and listen for the long-short.






Tip



Notice this lesson tracks separately from the quarter-note lesson — the report will show your time on each page, not just “lessons” as a lump.

title: LSA Practice

Work through Learning Sequence Activities on your own, continuously — a few class patterns to chant along with before each individual pattern, then your own turn, genuinely graded by ear (just like the rhythm assessment tool), but without showing the notation or the results. title: Evaluation Chart
text:
Learning Sequence Activities seating/evaluation chart. Two students are always cued — mark ① with Enter/Backspace and the next cued student is targeted automatically. Placeholder class of 6 — swap for the real roster once this is wired to Supabase.
